title: Saudações e o Alfabeto
subtitle: Greetings & the Alphabet
description: Learn how to greet people and introduce yourself in Portuguese, plus master the alphabet and basic sounds.

vocabulary.tip: Used at any time of day, very common and friendly
Used from morning until around noon
Used after sunset, both as a greeting and farewell
More formal goodbye, often used when parting for a long time
Casual farewell, implies you'll see them again soon
Men say 'obrigado', women say 'obrigada' — it agrees with the speaker's gender
Literally means 'of nothing' — the standard response to 'thank you'
Essential for polite requests; also 'faz favor' in Portugal

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Excuse me, where is the station?
vocabulary.tip: Use to get attention or apologize; 'desculpa' is the informal version

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My name is Ana. And you?
vocabulary.tip: Literally 'I call myself' — the reflexive verb chamar-se
Use 'de' before city names and most countries

vocabulary.example: Sim, eu falo português. Não, não falo espanhol.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Yes, I speak Portuguese. No, I don't speak Spanish.
vocabulary.tip: 'Não' has a nasal sound — practice the ão diphthong!

sections.heading: The Portuguese Alphabet
sections.body: Portuguese uses the same 26 letters as English, plus a few special characters: ç (c-cedilha), and letters with diacritical marks — à, á, â, ã, é, ê, í, ó, ô, õ, ú. The letters K, W, and Y exist but are almost exclusively used in foreign words and names. Each letter has a specific name: for example, A is 'á', B is 'bê', C is 'cê', and so on. The most important thing to learn at this stage is not the letter names, but the sounds — especially the nasal vowels, which don't exist in English.

sections.heading: What Are Nasal Vowels?
sections.body: Portuguese has a set of vowel sounds produced with air flowing through the nose. These are marked either with a tilde (~) above the vowel (like ã) or by the letters -m or -n in the same syllable (like in 'bem' or 'sim'). Mastering these sounds is one of the most important steps toward a natural Portuguese accent. Unlike English, where 'm' and 'n' are always pronounced as separate consonants, in Portuguese nasal vowels the vowel itself changes quality — the air flows through both the mouth and the nose simultaneously.

sections.heading: Nasal Vowel Sounds at a Glance
sections.caption: Compare each nasal sound to its closest English approximation

items.portuguese: Eu tenho uma mãe muito boa.
items.translation: I have a very good mother.
items.pronunciation: ay-oo TEN-yoo OO-mah may moo-ee-too BOH-ah

sections.heading: Oral vs. Nasal: Minimal Pairs
sections.description: These pairs show how a single nasal vowel can change the meaning of a word entirely.

pairs.note: Same spelling pattern, completely different meaning
The nasal version has a 'hazy' quality
Context usually makes the meaning clear

sections.heading: Common Mistakes & Tips
items: Do NOT pronounce the 'n' or 'm' as a separate consonant — let the vowel itself become nasal. In 'pão', you don't say 'pah-own', you say a single nasalized syllable.
The tilde (~) always means the vowel is nasal. If you see it, air goes through your nose.
In '-em' and '-en' endings, the 'e' changes sound entirely — it becomes a nasal diphthong, not a regular 'e'. 'Bem' doesn't sound like 'ben'.
Practice trick: hold your nose while saying a nasal vowel. If you feel vibration in your nostrils, you're doing it right.
The diphthong 'ão' is one of the hardest sounds for English speakers. Start by saying 'ow' (as in 'cow'), then slowly direct air through your nose while maintaining the mouth position.
Not every 'm' or 'n' creates a nasal vowel! In words like 'mesa' or 'cana', the vowels are oral. Nasal vowels only occur when m/n is in the same syllable and follows the vowel.

exercises.title: Identify the Nasal Vowel
exercises.instructions: Select the word that contains a nasal vowel sound.

items.question: Which word has a nasal vowel?
items.explanation: 'Sim' ends in '-im', which produces a nasal 'ee' sound. The others have regular oral vowels.

items.question: Which word does NOT have a nasal vowel?
items.explanation: 'Livro' has only oral vowels. 'Mão' has 'ão' (nasal), 'bem' has '-em' (nasal), and 'um' has '-um' (nasal).

items.question: Which word contains the nasal diphthong 'ão'?
items.explanation: 'Não' ends in 'ão', the most iconic Portuguese nasal sound. 'Com' has '-om', 'sim' has '-im', and 'um' has '-um'.

exercises.title: Complete the Nasal Vowel
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank with the correct nasal vowel spelling.
